prompt: Create a single-frame editorial image (not a standard chart) that embodies global sea-level rise as a personal, domestic threat: an empty child bedroom with a doorframe covered in year-by-year flood marks, where marks are small and faint in early decades and become longer, darker, and red-tinted in recent years to imply acceleration. Show seawater physically rising to the latest mark, flooding the room, partially submerging a teddy bear, and placing a child silhouette nearby for scale and emotional weight. No axis labels, no legend, no text, no infographic boxes, no multi-panel layout. Cinematic natural light from one side, muted earth palette with cold blue floodwater, high texture detail, magazine opener composition, 4:5 vertical, single image only.
dataset_name: Our World in Data - Global Mean Sea Level (Church and White 2011 + UHSLC average)
insight: Global sea level has risen dramatically since the late 19th century, and the rise is no longer abstract; it is visibly encroaching on human spaces.
concept: Translate long-run sea-level measurements into intimate household evidence by turning annual data points into physical flood marks on a child-room doorway.
metaphor: A flooded child bedroom where rising seawater climbs past yearly doorframe marks, making climate data feel like a direct domestic loss.
